reasoning:
Friedrich Merz was sworn in as Germany's Chancellor on May 6, 2025, after a snap election and subsequent coalition negotiations between the CDU/CSU and SPD. Search results up to early November 2025 (e.g., reports of him hosting international talks on November 4) confirm that he remains actively in power with no signs of an impending resignation, removal, or severe health crisis. 

The forecast horizon from the knowledge cutoff (November 9, 2025) to the resolution date (December 31, 2025) is only about seven weeks. In Germany's political system, removing a Chancellor requires a "constructive vote of no confidence," meaning the opposition must unite to elect a specific successor. Given the highly fractured state of the current opposition parties (which include the far-right AfD and left-wing factions), orchestrating this within the next seven weeks is virtually impossible. Furthermore, an early internal coalition collapse would most likely result in Merz staying on as acting Chancellor while new elections are organized. The only realistic paths for him to be out as Chancellor before the end of the year involve extreme "black swan" events, such as a sudden health emergency or a massive, unforeseen scandal that forces an immediate resignation. Consequently, the probability of him ceasing to be Chancellor before the end of 2025 is extremely low.
